system.","tr":"Stock"}],"lessonId":"ald-feedback-loops-0","mc":[{"ai":1,"ex":"Negative feedback counteracts deviations, helping to maintain stability around a set point or target.","fi":[0],"op":["Positive feedback","Negative feedback","Reinforcing loop","Exponential growth"],"q":"Which type of feedback mechanism is primarily responsible for stabilizing a system near a target value?"},{"ai":1,"ex":"Positive feedback amplifies changes, driving the system further away from its initial state, regardless of whether the change is beneficial.","fi":[1],"op":["It reduces the magnitude of changes over time.","It amplifies an initial change without judging its benefit.","It ensures the system reaches equilibrium quickly.","It prevents any further accumulation of stocks."],"q":"What is the primary characteristic of positive feedback in a system?"},{"ai":1,"ex":"A delayed response can cause corrections to be applied after the system has already moved past the The constraint is the limited budget, forcing a choice between short-term relief via road expansion and long-term stability via alternative transport systems.","su":"A city planner is addressing chronic traffic congestion on a major highway. The planner must decide whether to proceed with the expansion or invest in public transit alternatives, considering a strict budget constraint that allows for only one major infrastructure investment this decade."},"studyGuide":{"rs":"Remember that causal-loop diagrams illustrate relationships and directions of influence but do not quantify the magnitude of effects. the methods must be evaluated directly.","df":"The quality of the research design and execution, which determines the validity of the conclusions drawn.","eg":"Using a randomized control group to isolate the effect of a new drug.","mi":"If a study is published in a prestigious journal, its methodology is guaranteed to be sound.","tr":"Methodological Soundness"}],"lessonId":"ald-source-evaluation-1","mc":[{"ai":0,"ex":"Relevance to the specific population and outcome strengthens the applicability of the evidence to the claim.","fi":[0],"op":["It ensures the evidence is relevant to the specific context of the claim.","It guarantees that the publication venue is prestigious.","It allows the researcher to ignore secondary sources entirely.","It proves that no uncertainty exists in the findings."],"q":"Which statement best describes why a claim is stronger when cited evidence directly addresses the same population and outcome?"},{"ai":0,"ex":"Publication venue alone cannot establish that a specific claim is methodologically sound; algorithms?"},{"ai":3,"ex":"Benchmark results depend on implementation, hardware, input distribution, and measurement procedure, not just the abstract complexity class.","fi":[4],"op":["Implementation facts?"},{"ai":1,"ex":"Worst-case complexity bounds the maximum work over inputs of a given size.","fi":[3],"op":["The minimum work required for any input.","The maximum work over inputs of a given must choose between implementing a simple linear scan or sorting the data to enable binary search. Observation 1: The datasets contain fewer than 20 elements. Observation 2: Sorting introduces overhead that exceeds the time saved by binary search on such small sets. Constraint: The system cannot afford the latency of pre-sorting operations at runtime.","su":"Selecting a search strategy for a legacy system with small, unsorted lookup tables."},"studyGuide":{"rs":"Practice identifying when to use theoretical analysis versus empirical benchmarking. Understand why linear scans can outperform binary search on small inputs due to constant factors and ordering requirements."}}]}

{"lessons":[{"assignmentCore":{"pa":["Target Audience: High School Biology Students","Topic: Cellular Respiration Pathways","Format: Peer-Review Worksheet with Guided Prompts","Constraint: Must include at least one retrieval practice component"],"td":"Design a formative assessment activity that includes specific feedback mechanisms to help students identify gaps in their understanding of a complex topic."},"discussionPrompt":{"po":["Scores provide necessary accountability and benchmarking, while feedback should be secondary.","Feedback identifying gaps and next steps is essential for learning, making scores largely irrelevant for improvement.","Scores and feedback must be integrated, as one assessment result should be interpreted with task conditions and corroborating evidence."],"pr":"How should educators balance the provision of numerical scores with qualitative feedback to maximize student improvement?","tn":"The tension between the administrative need for quantifiable metrics and the pedagogical need for descriptive guidance."},"facts":["Formative evidence informs a next instructional or learning move before final evaluation.","Feedback is more actionable when it identifies a gap and a feasible next step.","A score alone reports performance but does not explain how to improve it.","Retrieval practice can reveal durable recall more directly than rereading familiarity.","One assessment result should be interpreted with task conditions and corroborating evidence."],"keyTerms":[{"cx":"Formative evidence informs a next instructional or learning move before final evaluation, not for summative grading.","df":"Data collected during the learning process to guide immediate instructional adjustments before any final grading occurs.","eg":"Using exit tickets to decide whether to review a concept the next day.","mi":"Formative evidence is primarily used to assign grades at the end of a unit.","tr":"Formative Evidence"},{"cx":"Feedback is more actionable when it identifies a gap and a feasible next step, whereas a score alone reports performance but does not explain how to improve it.","df":"Specific guidance that pinpoints a performance deficit and provides a clear, achievable method for the learner to address it.","eg":"Telling a student their thesis lacks evidence and suggesting they add two citations.","mi":"Providing a detailed rubric score is sufficient for actionable feedback.","tr":"Actionable Feedback"},{"cx":"Retrieval practice can reveal durable recall more directly than rereading familiarity, making it superior for long-term retention.","df":"A learning technique involving active recall of information from memory rather than passive re-exposure to the material.","eg":"Taking a practice quiz without notes instead of highlighting the textbook again.","mi":"Rereading notes is the most effective way to ensure long-term retention of facts.","tr":"Retrieval Practice"}],"lessonId":"ald-formative-feedback-3","mc":[{"ai":1,"ex":"Formative evidence is used during the learning process to guide adjustments, not for final grading.","fi":[0],"op":["To assign a final grade for the semester","To inform a next instructional or learning move before final evaluation","To compare student performance across different schools","To document compliance with state testing standards"],"q":"What is the primary purpose of formative evidence in an instructional setting?"},{"ai":1,"ex":"A score indicates where a student stands but lacks the descriptive detail needed for growth.","fi":[2],"op":["It takes too long to calculate accurately","It reports performance but does not explain how to improve it","Students generally do not understand numerical values","It requires expensive software to generate"],"q":"Why is a score alone often insufficient for helping a student improve?"},{"ai":1,"ex":"Actionable feedback must provide clear direction on what is missing and how to fix it.","fi":[1],"op":["It is delivered verbally in front of the class","It identifies a gap and a feasible next step","It focuses solely on the highest possible grade","It uses complex academic jargon to sound authoritative"],"q":"Which characteristic makes feedback more actionable for learners?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"Context and additional data are necessary to understand what an assessment result truly signifies.","fi":[4],"op":["As the definitive measure of a student's intelligence","In isolation without considering other factors","With task conditions and corroborating evidence","Only if the student agrees with the score"],"q":"How should a single assessment result be interpreted for accurate evaluation?"}],"scenario":{"ma":"The teacher observes that many students confused glycolysis with the Krebs cycle (Observation 1) and notes that these students had high homework completion rates but low quiz scores (Observation 2). The constraint is that there are only two class periods left before the final exam, so the teacher cannot reteach the entire unit from scratch.","su":"A teacher reviews mid-semester quiz results where 60% of students missed questions on cellular respiration. The teacher must decide how to proceed before the final exam."},"studyGuide":{"rs":"Use retrieval practice by writing down three examples of actionable feedback from memory before checking your notes.","sm":"Review the definitions of formative vs. summative assessment and practice creating feedback that includes both a gap identification and a next step."}}]}

{"lessons":[{"assignmentCore":{"pa":["Target Audience: Novice Learners","Procedure Complexity: Moderate","Support Type: Visual and Textual Hints","Assessment Method: Performance practice."},"discussionPrompt":{"po":["Worked examples should be used exclusively until mastery is proven, as any independent attempt too early increases extraneous load and causes failure.","Support should fade immediately after the first successful attempt to prevent dependency and force the learner to engage deeply with the material.","A gradual fading approach is optimal, where support is reduced incrementally based on demonstrated competence to manage intrinsic load while building independence."],"pr":"How should instructors balance the provision of worked examples with the need for independent practice to optimize cognitive load?","tn":"The Fading Support Dilemma"},"facts":["Working memory has limited capacity for simultaneously processed novel information.","Relevant prior knowledge can organize several details into one meaningful mental chunk.","Extraneous complexity consumes processing capacity without advancing the learning goal.","A worked example can reduce unnecessary search while a learner acquires a procedure.","Support should fade as learners demonstrate that they can perform the process independently."],"keyTerms":[{"cx":"Excessive cognitive load overwhelms working memory capacity, hindering the ability to process and retain new information effectively.","df":"The total amount of mental effort being used in working memory at any given time increasing competence and independence.","eg":"Removing step-by-step hints from a software tutorial after the user completes several tasks correctly.","mi":"Supports should remain constant throughout information.","Working memory capacity increases significantly during the first week of learning."],"q":"Why is it important to consider working memory capacity when designing instructional materials?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"Fact 1 explains that relevant prior knowledge can organize several details into one meaningful mental chunk, thereby reducing the load on working memory.","fi":[1],"op":["It eliminates the need for any new learning.","It allows learners to ignore all new information.","Relevant prior knowledge can organize several details into one meaningful mental chunk.","It increases the extraneous complexity of the task."],"q":"How does prior knowledge assist in managing cognitive load?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"Fact 2 states that extraneous complexity consumes processing capacity without advancing the learning goal, which can hinder effective learning.","fi":[2],"op":["They enhance the aesthetic appeal without affecting learning.","They help students memorize facts through association.","Extraneous complexity consumes processing capacity without advancing the learning goal.","They reduce the intrinsic load of the material."],"q":"What is the primary risk of including for less guidance.","fi":[4],"op":["Never, as learners always need full support.","Immediately after the first exposure to the material.","Only when the learner fails completely.","Support should fade and subgroup patterns.","Interactive dashboards allow users to toggle between aggregated views and individual observations, solving the trade-off."],"pr":"How should designers balance the need for visual comparisons but do not change the underlying observations.","Position on a common scale is generally easier to compare accurately than area or volume.","A legend maps visual encodings to distinctions that do not rely on color alone."],"keyTerms":[{"cx":"Visual encoding is the systematic mapping of data variables to visual channels like position or size, not just aesthetic color choices.","df":"The graphical representation of data values using visual properties such as position, length, angle, or color within a chart.","eg":"Using bar height to represent sales figures in a column chart.","mi":"Visual encoding is simply the act of choosing colors for a graph to make it look appealing.","tr":"Visual Encoding"},{"cx":"Aggregation can conceal variation and subgroup patterns present in individual observations, potentially hiding important outliers or trends.","df":"The process of combining individual data points into summary statistics, which may hide specific details within the dataset.","eg":"Calculating the average test score for a class instead of listing every student's score.","mi":"Aggregation always provides a more accurate picture of the data than looking at individual records.","tr":"Aggregation"},{"cx":"An accessible chart pairs meaningful labels with distinctions that do not rely on color alone, ensuring usability for those with color vision deficiencies.","df":"A data visualization designed to be perceivable by users with various abilities, often using labels and non-color distinctions.","eg":"A line chart that uses different line styles (dashed, solid) and text labels in addition to colors.","mi":"An accessible chart is one that is easy to read because it has large fonts and bright colors.","tr":"Accessible Chart"}],"lessonId":"ald-data-visualization-5","mc":[{"ai":0,"ex":"Human perception is more accurate at judging linear distances along an axis than relative areas.","fi":[1],"op":["Position is calculate than position."],"q":"Why is position on a common scale preferred over area for comparing values?"},{"ai":0,"ex":"Legends provide the key to interpreting what specific colors, shapes, or observations.","Aggregation makes the chart too large to view on a screen.","Aggregation changes the axis limits automatically.","Aggregation requires more color distinctions."],"q":"What is a {"lessons":[{"assignmentCore":{"pa":["Identify producers","Map feeding relationships","Calculate energy loss","Analyze species impact"],"td":"Construct a detailed food web diagram for a local ecosystem, identifying at least three trophic levels and explaining how energy dissipates at each transfer."},"discussionPrompt":{"po":["Remove the fish immediately to protect native amphibian populations and restore original food web structures.","Do not remove the fish because removing one species can affect several populations through direct and indirect relationships, potentially causing unforeseen collapses.","Monitor the system first, as biomass changes reflect complex interactions of production and consumption that need time to stabilize before acting."],"pr":"Should the management team proceed with the removal of the invasive predatory fish?","tn":"Evaluate the ecological stability and energy flow implications of intervention versus non-intervention."},"facts":["Energy enters most ecosystems when producers transform sunlight into stored chemical energy.","Matter cycles through ecosystems, whereas usable energy dissipates as heat during transfers.","A food web represents multiple feeding relationships rather than one isolated linear chain.","Removing one species can affect several populations through direct and indirect relationships.","Biomass at a trophic level reflects production, consumption, respiration, and time scale."],"keyTerms":[{"cx":"Usable energy dissipates as heat during transfers between trophic levels, meaning energy is not perfectly conserved.","df":"A position in a food chain or ecological pyramid occupied by an organism that feeds on the level below it.","eg":"Primary consumers occupy the second trophic level by eating producers.","mi":"Trophic levels represent static storage units where energy is perfectly conserved without loss.","tr":"Trophic Level"},{"cx":"A food web represents multiple feeding relationships rather than one isolated linear chain.","df":"A complex network of interconnected food chains showing the flow of energy and matter through an ecosystem.","eg":"A forest food web includes deer eating plants, wolves eating deer, and fungi decomposing both.","mi":"A food web depicts only single, linear pathways of energy transfer from producer to top predator.","tr":"Food Web"},{"cx":"Biomass at a trophic level reflects production, consumption, respiration, and time scale, not just sunlight.","df":"The total mass of living biological organisms in a given area or ecosystem at a specific time.","eg":"The biomass of phytoplankton in a lake is measured to estimate primary production capacity.","mi":"Biomass is determined solely by the amount of sunlight received, ignoring metabolic processes.","tr":"Biomass"}],"lessonId":"ald-ecosystem-energy-6","mc":[{"ai":1,"ex":"This aligns with the fact that energy enters most ecosystems when producers transform sunlight into stored chemical energy.","fi":[0],"op":["Through the decomposition of dead organic matter by fungi.","When producers transform sunlight into stored chemical energy.","Via the consumption of inorganic minerals by primary consumers.","By the absorption of heat from the Earth's core by plants."],"q":"How does energy enter most ecosystems?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"Matter cycles through ecosystems, whereas usable energy dissipates as heat during transfers.","fi":[1],"op":["It cycles continuously without any loss or change in form.","It is converted entirely into biomass at each trophic level.","It dissipates as heat during transfers between organisms.","It accumulates in the soil and is reused by producers directly."],"q":"What happens to usable energy as it moves through an ecosystem?"},{"ai":0,"ex":"A food web represents multiple feeding relationships rather than one isolated linear chain.","fi":[2],"op":["Because it represents multiple feeding relationships rather than one isolated linear chain.","Because it only includes herbivores and ignores carnivores.","Because it shows that energy does not dissipate as heat.","Because it proves that removing species has no effect on populations."],"q":"Why is a food web considered more accurate than a single food chain?"},{"ai":1,"ex":"Biomass at a trophic level reflects production, consumption, respiration, and time scale.","fi":[4],"op":["Only the amount of sunlight available to the producers.","Production, consumption, respiration, and time scale.","The number of predators present in the ecosystem.","The total mass of non-living matter in the environment."],"q":"What factors determine the biomass at a specific trophic level?"}],"scenario":{"ma":"Observation 1: The invasive fish preys heavily on native amphibian larvae. Observation 2: Native amphibians are key predators of mosquito larvae. Constraint: Removal efforts are costly and may disturb sediment, releasing stored nutrients.","su":"A wetland management team must decide whether to remove an invasive predatory fish species that has recently established itself in the ecosystem."},"studyGuide":{"rs":"Focus on how removing a single species can ripple through direct and indirect relationships. Memorize that energy enters via producers and dissipates as heat. Relate biomass to production and respiration over time.","sm":"Review the distinction between matter cycling and energy dissipation. Practice drawing food webs to visualize indirect relationships. Understand how biomass is calculated based on metabolic rates."}}]}

{"lessons":[{"assignmentCore":{"pa":["Identify two stocks","Map three flows","Label one reinforcing loop","Label one balancing loop"],"td":"Create a causal-loop diagram for a local ecosystem showing predator-prey dynamics and identify feedback types."},"discussionPrompt":{"po":["Delays are inherently destabilizing and should be minimized at all costs to prevent overshooting.","Some delay is necessary to filter out noise and prevent overreaction to minor, temporary fluctuations.","The magnitude of the delay matters less than the strength of the corrective action applied."],"pr":"How do delays in feedback loops impact the stability of complex systems?","tn":"Analyzing the role of timing in corrective actions within systemic models."},"facts":["Negative feedback counteracts a deviation and can stabilize a system near a target.","Positive feedback amplifies an initial change rather than judging the change as beneficial.","A delayed response can cause correction to overshoot the target repeatedly.","A stock accumulates the difference between incoming and outgoing flows over time.","A causal-loop diagram marks reinforcing and balancing relationships without proving magnitude."],"keyTerms":[{"cx":"It allows small fluctuations but actively corrects significant deviations to maintain stability.","df":"A mechanism that counteracts deviations to stabilize a system near a specific target value.","eg":"A thermostat turning off the heater when the room reaches the set temperature.","mi":"It always prevents any change from occurring in the system.","tr":"Negative Feedback"},{"cx":"It simply amplifies the change regardless of whether the outcome is good or bad.","df":"A process that amplifies an initial change, driving the system further away from its starting state.","eg":"Microphone audio feedback where sound is picked up and re-amplified louder.","mi":"It indicates that the change is beneficial or desirable for the system.","tr":"Positive Feedback"},{"cx":"It is dynamic and changes continuously based on the balance of inflows and outflows.","df":"An accumulation that represents the net difference between incoming and outgoing flows over time.","eg":"The water level in a bathtub rising as inflow exceeds outflow.","mi":"It is a static quantity that does not change once established.","tr":"Stock"}],"lessonId":"ald-feedback-loops-8","mc":[{"ai":1,"ex":"Negative feedback counteracts deviations to maintain stability.","fi":[0],"op":["Positive feedback","Negative feedback","Delayed response","Causal-loop diagram"],"q":"Which mechanism helps stabilize a system near a target value?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"Positive feedback amplifies changes rather than judging them.","fi":[1],"op":["It stabilizes the system near a target.","It accumulates differences over time.","It amplifies an initial change.","It proves the magnitude of relationships."],"q":"What characterizes positive feedback in a system?"},{"ai":1,"ex":"Delays cause corrections to arrive too late, leading to overshooting.","fi":[2],"op":["Due to negative feedback","Because of a delayed response","Due to positive feedback","Because stocks accumulate flows"],"q":"Why might a correction overshoot the target repeatedly?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"A stock accumulates the difference between incoming and outgoing flows.","fi":[3],"op":["As a flow that moves instantly","As a diagram marking relationships","As an accumulation of net flows over time","As a mechanism that amplifies change"],"q":"How is a stock defined in systems thinking?"}],"scenario":{"ma":"The planner must decide how quickly the lights should respond to detected car queues. Observation 1: Immediate response causes lights to flicker chaotically. Observation 2: Slow response leads to massive backups. Constraint: The system must avoid oscillating wildly while preventing gridlock.","su":"A city planner is designing a traffic light timing system for a busy intersection to reduce congestion."},"studyGuide":{"rs":"Focus on distinguishing between stocks (accumulations) and flows (rates). Remember that causal-loop diagrams show relationships but do not prove magnitude.","sm":"Review the definitions of negative and positive feedback and how delays affect system stability. Practice drawing causal-loop diagrams to visualize reinforcing and balancing relationships."}}]}

{"lessons":[{"assignmentCore":{"pa":["Identify source type (primary/secondary)","Check population alignment","Assess independence of evidence","State remaining uncertainties"],"td":"Analyze two provided sources to determine if they corroborate each other and identify any methodological gaps."},"discussionPrompt":{"po":["Researchers should withhold judgment and explicitly state the uncertainty rather than forcing a conclusion.","Researchers should favor the explanation supported by the most prestigious publication venues.","Researchers should combine secondary interpretations to create a consensus view despite lack of direct evidence."],"pr":"How should researchers handle situations where available evidence is insufficient to distinguish between competing explanations?","tn":"Evaluating Evidence Under Uncertainty"},"facts":["A claim is stronger when cited evidence directly addresses the same population and outcome.","Publication venue alone cannot establish that a specific claim is methodologically sound.","A primary source records direct evidence, while a secondary source interprets other records.","Corroboration compares independent evidence that bears on the same factual claim.","Uncertainty should be stated when available evidence cannot distinguish competing explanations."],"keyTerms":[{"cx":"A journal article may be a secondary source if it reviews or interprets other studies rather than presenting new data.","df":"A document or record that provides direct, first-hand evidence of an event or phenomenon without interpretation.","eg":"A raw dataset from a clinical trial or a diary entry from a historical figure.","mi":"Any published article in a peer-reviewed journal is automatically a primary source.","tr":"Primary Source"},{"cx":"True corroboration requires independent evidence; relying on the same source chain does not verify the claim.","df":"The process of comparing independent pieces of evidence to see if they support the same factual claim.","eg":"Checking if satellite imagery matches eyewitness accounts of a natural disaster.","mi":"Citing multiple sources that all rely on the same original report constitutes strong corroboration.","tr":"Corroboration"},{"cx":"Publication venue alone cannot establish that a specific claim is methodologically sound; the methods must be evaluated directly.","df":"The quality of the research design and execution, which determines the validity of the conclusions drawn.","eg":"Using a randomized control group to isolate the effect of a new drug.","mi":"If a study is published in a prestigious journal, its methodology is guaranteed to be sound.","tr":"Methodological Soundness"}],"lessonId":"ald-source-evaluation-9","mc":[{"ai":0,"ex":"A claim is stronger when cited evidence directly addresses the same population and outcome.","fi":[0],"op":["Citing evidence that smaller studies."],"q":"Which action best strengthens a claim regarding a specific health outcome?"},{"ai":0,"ex":"Publication venue alone cannot establish that a specific claim is methodologically sound.","fi":[1],"op":["It cannot establish that a specific claim is methodologically sound.","It prevents the use of primary sources.","It eliminates the need for corroboration.","It guarantees the absence of uncertainty."],"q":"What is the primary limitation of relying solely on publication venue?"},{"ai":0,"ex":"A primary source records direct evidence, while a secondary source interprets other records.","fi":[2],"op":["A primary source records direct evidence, while a secondary source interprets other records.","A primary source is always more recent than a secondary source.","A primary source is published in journals, while secondary sources are books.","A primary source requires corroboration, while a secondary source does not."],"q":"How does a primary source differ from a secondary source?"},{"ai":0,"ex":"Corroboration compares independent evidence that bears on the same factual claim.","fi":[3],"op":["Comparing independent evidence that bears on the same factual claim.","Repeating the same source in multiple citations.","Choosing the most popular explanation among experts.","Ignoring evidence that contradicts the initial hypothesis."],"q":"What is the correct definition of corroboration?"}],"scenario":{"ma":"Observation 1: A high-impact journal published a study supporting the initiative. Observation 2: A primary data audit reveals the study's sample population differs significantly from the target demographic. Constraint: The team must justify their decision using only the provided evidence types.","su":"A policy team must decide whether to fund a new public health initiative based on conflicting reports."},"studyGuide":{"rs":"Focus on how to articulate uncertainty when evidence is inconclusive and how to verify independence in corroborating sources.","sm":"Review the definitions of primary vs. secondary sources and practice identifying methodological flaws in sample populations."}}]}

{"lessons":[{"assignmentCore":{"pa":["input_sizes","sorted_data_flag","measurement_unit","output_format"],"td":"Implement both linear and binary search algorithms and generate a comparative performance report for varying input sizes."},"discussionPrompt":{"po":["Asymptotic analysis is sufficient for all production decisions because constant factors are negligible at scale.","Benchmarking is essential because hardware and implementation details often dominate performance for realistic input sizes.","A hybrid approach is best, using asymptotic analysis for scaling predictions and benchmarks for constant-factor optimization."],"pr":"When should engineers prioritize empirical benchmarking over theoretical asymptotic analysis?","tn":"The tension between theoretical guarantees and practical performance realities."},"facts":["Asymptotic analysis compares growth rates while ignoring constant factors at sufficiently large input sizes.","Binary search requires an ordered search space and halves the remaining interval each step.","A linear scan may outperform binary search on tiny inputs despite its less favorable growth rate.","Worst-case complexity bounds the maximum work over inputs of a given size.","Benchmark results depend on implementation, hardware, input distribution, and measurement procedure."],"keyTerms":[{"cx":"It ignores constant factors and only describes behavior at sufficiently large input sizes.","df":"A method for classifying algorithms by their growth rates as input size approaches infinity, disregarding constant overheads.","eg":"Comparing O(n) vs O(log n) to predict performance scaling for massive datasets.","mi":"It provides exact execution times for any specific input size on a given machine.","tr":"Asymptotic Analysis"},{"cx":"It requires an ordered search space and may be slower than linear scan on tiny inputs.","df":"An efficient search algorithm that repeatedly divides a sorted search space in half to locate a target value.","eg":"Finding a word in a dictionary by opening to the middle and discarding half the pages.","mi":"It is always faster than linear search regardless of the dataset size or structure.","tr":"Binary Search"},{"cx":"It bounds the maximum work over inputs of a given size, not the average case.","df":"The upper bound on the computational resources required by an algorithm for any input of a specified size.","eg":"Analyzing the maximum number of comparisons needed to sort a reverse-sorted array.","mi":"It represents the average performance expected during typical usage scenarios.","tr":"Worst-Case Complexity"}],"lessonId":"ald-algorithmic-complexity-10","mc":[{"ai":1,"ex":"Asymptotic analysis abstracts away constant factors to focus on how resource requirements grow with input size.","fi":[0],"op":["It calculates exact execution time in milliseconds for any input.","It compares growth rates while ignoring constant factors at large input sizes.","It measures the memory usage of an algorithm on specific hardware.","It determines the average case performance across all possible inputs."],"q":"Which statement accurately describes the primary focus of asymptotic analysis?"},{"ai":1,"ex":"Binary search relies on the ability to eliminate half the remaining interval, which requires an ordered search space.","fi":[1],"op":["The data must be stored in a linked list structure.","The search space must be ordered and halved each step.","The input size must be larger than one million elements.","The algorithm must run on parallel processing hardware."],"q":"What is a strict prerequisite for applying binary search effectively?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"For very small inputs, the overhead of binary search logic can exceed the simple iteration cost of a linear scan.","fi":[2],"op":["Linear scan has a better worst-case complexity bound.","Linear scan does not require the data to be sorted beforehand.","Linear scan may outperform binary search on tiny inputs despite worse growth rate.","Linear scan is immune to hardware-dependent benchmark variations."],"q":"Why might a linear scan be preferred over binary search in certain contexts?"},{"ai":1,"ex":"Worst-case complexity provides a guarantee on the upper bound of resource usage for any input of that size.","fi":[3],"op":["The minimum work required for the easiest possible input.","The maximum work over inputs of a given size.","The average work across a uniform distribution of inputs.","The total energy consumption of the processor during execution."],"q":"What does worst-case complexity specifically quantify?"}],"scenario":{"ma":"Observation 1: Binary search has a better asymptotic growth rate. Observation 2: The input size is extremely small. Constraint: The system has strict latency requirements for this specific micro-operation.","su":"A developer must choose between binary search and linear scan for a lookup table containing 10 elements."},"studyGuide":{"rs":"Practice deriving growth rates for simple loops. Compare theoretical O-notation with actual runtime measurements for small vs large N to observe the impact of constant factors.","sm":"Review the definitions of asymptotic analysis and worst-case complexity. Understand the trade-offs between algorithmic efficiency and constant factors in small datasets."}}]}

{"lessons":[{"assignmentCore":{"pa":["Identify one common misconception from the quiz","Provide a specific retrieval practice activity","Explain why the score alone is insufficient","Suggest one feasible next step for improvement"],"td":"Design a one-page feedback guide for students that explains how to interpret their quiz scores and identify next steps."},"discussionPrompt":{"po":["Prioritize speed by using automated scoring to provide immediate formative evidence, accepting that some nuance is lost.","Prioritize depth by providing detailed written feedback on fewer assignments to ensure students understand their gaps.","Use peer review systems to distribute the workload while maintaining high-quality, actionable feedback for all students."],"pr":"How should educators balance the need for quick assessment data with the requirement for deep, actionable feedback?","tn":"The tension between efficiency in grading and the pedagogical value of detailed, gap-identifying feedback."},"facts":["Formative evidence informs a next instructional or learning move before final evaluation.","Feedback is more actionable when it identifies a gap and a feasible next step.","A score alone reports performance but does not explain how to improve it.","Retrieval practice can reveal durable recall more directly than rereading familiarity.","One assessment result should be interpreted with task conditions and corroborating evidence."],"keyTerms":[{"cx":"Formative evidence informs a next instructional or learning move before final evaluation, not for summative grading.","df":"Data collected during the learning process to guide immediate instructional adjustments before any final grading occurs.","eg":"Using exit tickets to decide whether to review a concept the next day.","mi":"Formative evidence is primarily used to assign grades at the end of a unit.","tr":"Formative Evidence"},{"cx":"Feedback is more actionable when it identifies a gap and a feasible next step, rather than just listing errors.","df":"Specific guidance that pinpoints a performance deficit and provides a clear, achievable method for the actionable feedback.","tr":"Actionable Feedback"},{"cx":"Retrieval practice can reveal durable recall more directly than rereading familiarity, which often creates an illusion of competence.","df":"A attendance and participation"],"q":"What is the primary purpose of formative evidence according to the source facts?"},{"ai":0,"ex":"Fact 2 explicitly states that a score alone reports performance but does not explain how to improve it.","fi":[2],"op":["It does not explain how to improve it","It is always inaccurate due to grading errors","It focuses too much on qualitative data","It is only useful for summative assessments"],"q":"Why is a score alone often considered insufficient for improving performance?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"Fact 4 indicates that retrieval practice can reveal durable recall more directly than rereading familiarity.","fi":[3],"op":["Highlighting key terms in the text","Summarizing chapters in bullet points","Retrieval practice","Listening to audio recordings of {"lessons":[{"assignmentCore":{"pa":["Include a legend mapping colors to product names","Set axis limits from zero to the maximum value","Add text labels to each bar for exact values","Use distinct patterns for color-blind accessibility"],"td":"Create a static bar chart comparing quarterly revenue for three product lines, ensuring all bars are labeled directly and no color-only distinctions are used."},"discussionPrompt":{"po":["Prioritize aggregation to reduce cognitive load, accepting that some subgroup patterns will be concealed.","Avoid aggregation entirely to preserve all individual observations, even if it makes the display cluttered.","Use interactive layers or supplementary tables to show aggregated views while allowing users to drill down into raw data."],"pr":"How should designers balance the need for visual simplicity with the risk of hiding important data variations?","tn":"The tension between clear communication and comprehensive data representation."},"facts":["Axis limits influence visual comparisons but do not change the underlying observations.","Position on a common scale is generally easier to compare accurately than area or volume.","A legend maps visual encodings to variables and categories represented in a display.","Aggregation can conceal variation and subgroup patterns present in individual observations.","An accessible chart pairs meaningful labels with distinctions that do not rely on color alone."],"keyTerms":[{"cx":"Visual encoding is the systematic mapping of data variables to visual channels like position or size, not just aesthetic color choices.","df":"The graphical representation of data values using visual properties such as position, length, angle, or color within a chart.","eg":"Using bar height to represent sales figures in a column chart.","mi":"Visual encoding is simply the act of choosing colors for a graph to make it look appealing.","tr":"Visual Encoding"},{"cx":"Aggregation can conceal variation and subgroup patterns present in individual observations, potentially hiding important outliers or trends.","df":"The process of combining individual data points into summary statistics, which may hide specific details within the dataset.","eg":"Calculating the average test score for a class instead of listing every student's score.","mi":"Aggregation always provides a more accurate picture of the data than looking at individual records.","tr":"Aggregation"},{"cx":"An accessible chart pairs meaningful labels with distinctions that do not rely on color alone, ensuring usability for those with color vision deficiencies.","df":"A data visualization designed to be perceivable by users with various abilities, often using labels and non-color distinctions.","eg":"A line chart that uses different line styles (dashed, solid) and text labels in addition to colors.","mi":"An accessible chart is one that is easy to read because it has large fonts and bright colors.","tr":"Accessible Chart"}],"lessonId":"ald-data-visualization-13","mc":[{"ai":0,"ex":"Human perception is more accurate at judging linear distances along an axis than relative areas.","fi":[1],"op":["Position is generally easier to compare accurately than area or volume.","Area charts are always misleading and should never be used.","Position requires less ink to draw than area.","Volume is harder to calculate than position."],"q":"Why is position on a common scale preferred over area for comparing values?"},{"ai":0,"ex":"Summarizing data may hide important details or outliers that exist within the raw dataset.","fi":[3],"op":["It can conceal variation and subgroup patterns present in individual observations.","It makes the chart too large to fit on a screen.","It changes the underlying observations to incorrect values.","It removes the need for a legend entirely."],"q":"What is a potential downside of aggregating data in a visualization?"},{"ai":0,"ex":"Using labels and patterns ensures that information is conveyed even if color differences are not perceived.","fi":[4],"op":["It pairs meaningful labels with distinctions that do not rely on color alone.","It uses only black and white colors for all elements.","It avoids using any visual encodings whatsoever.","It relies exclusively on color to distinguish categories."],"q":"How does an accessible chart ensure usability for users with color vision deficiencies?"},{"ai":0,"ex":"The legend acts as a key, explaining what each visual element (like color or shape) represents.","fi":[2],"op":["It maps visual encodings to variables and categories represented in a display.","It changes the axis limits to improve visual comparison.","It aggregates individual observations into summary statistics.","It provides the title and author of the chart."],"q":"What is the function of a legend in a data display?"}],"scenario":{"ma":"The analyst decides to use a bar chart where position on a common scale allows for accurate comparison of rates. To ensure accessibility, they add direct labels to each bar and use distinct patterns rather than relying solely on color. They also avoid aggregating data into broad national averages to prevent concealing significant variation between specific neighborhoods.","su":"A public health analyst must present regional vaccination rates to a diverse community board that includes members with color blindness."},"studyGuide":{"rs":"Focus on understanding how aggregation impacts data visibility and how axis limits influence perception without altering facts.","sm":"Review how visual encodings like position and area affect accuracy, and practice creating charts that do not rely solely on color for distinction."}}]}

{"lessons":[{"assignmentCore":{"pa":["Include at least five distinct species","Label all energy flow directions","Identify one potential point of failure","Explain one indirect transfer."},"discussionPrompt":{"po":["Immediate removal is necessary because the direct predation pressure on amphibians threatens their extinction, outweighing risks to invertebrates.","Removal should be delayed until more data is gathered, as removing the fish might cause an unexpected boom in invertebrate populations that disrupts water quality.","The focus should shift to restoring habitat complexity rather than removal, as food webs are resilient and the system may adapt to the new predator over time."],"pr":"How should the management team weigh the immediate benefits of removing the invasive predator against the potential indirect effects on other populations?","tn":"Ecosystem Stability and Indirect Effects"},"facts":["Energy enters most ecosystems when producers transform sunlight into stored chemical energy.","Matter cycles through ecosystems, whereas usable energy dissipates as heat during transfers.","A food web represents multiple feeding relationships rather than one isolated linear chain.","Removing one species can affect several populations through direct and indirect relationships.","Biomass at a trophic level reflects production, consumption, respiration, and time scale."],"keyTerms":[{"cx":"Trophic levels are functional positions based on current feeding relationships, which can vary by life stage or context.","df":"A position in a food chain or ecological pyramid occupied by an organism that feeds on the level below it.","eg":"Primary consumers occupy the second trophic level by eating producers.","mi":"Trophic levels represent static groups of species that never change their feeding habits.","tr":"Trophic Level"},{"cx":"A food web represents multiple overlapping feeding relationships rather than one isolated linear chain.","df":"A complex network of interconnected food chains showing the flow of energy and matter through an ecosystem.","eg":"A forest food web includes deer eating plants, wolves eating deer, and fungi decomposing both.","mi":"A food web is simply a longer version of a single linear food chain with more steps.","tr":"Food Web"},{"cx":"Biomass reflects production, consumption, respiration, and time scale, not just organism count.","df":"The total mass of living biological organisms in a given area or ecosystem at a specific time.","eg":"The biomass of phytoplankton in a lake is measured to estimate primary production capacity.","mi":"Biomass is solely determined by the number of individual organisms present in a population.","tr":"Biomass"}],"lessonId":"ald-ecosystem-energy-14","mc":[{"ai":2,"ex":"This directly reflects the principle that matter is conserved and cycled, but energy quality degrades into heat.","fi":[1],"op":["Both energy and matter cycle continuously without loss.","Energy cycles while matter dissipates as heat.","Matter cycles through ecosystems, whereas usable energy dissipates as heat during transfers.","Energy is created by consumers and matter is destroyed by producers."],"q":"Which statement best describes the flow of energy in an ecosystem compared to matter?"},{"ai":1,"ex":"The definition of a food web emphasizes its complexity and interconnectedness over linear simplicity.","fi":[2],"op":["Food webs only include plants and ignore animals.","A food web represents multiple feeding relationships rather than one isolated linear chain.","Food chains are too complex for most ecosystems to support.","Food webs show that energy increases at each trophic level."],"q":"Why is a food web considered a more accurate representation of an ecosystem than a single food chain?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"Producers initiate the energy flow by converting solar radiation into chemical bonds.","fi":[0],"op":["Chemical energy stored in fossil fuels.","Heat generated by geothermal activity.","Sunlight transformed into stored chemical energy by producers.","Kinetic energy from wind and water currents."],"q":"What is the primary source of energy for most ecosystems?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"Ecological networks are interconnected; changes in one node ripple through the system via various pathways.","fi":[3],"op":["No other species will be affected because they do not interact directly.","Only the prey population will increase, with no further effects.","Removing one species can affect several populations through direct and indirect relationships.","Energy will begin to cycle instead of dissipating as heat."],"q":"If a top predator is removed from an ecosystem, what is a likely consequence based on ecological principles?"}],"scenario":{"ma":"Observation 1: The invasive fish preys heavily on native amphibian larvae. Observation 2: Native amphibians are key prey for several bird species. Constraint: Removal methods may also harm non-target bottom-dwelling invertebrates.","su":"A wetland management team must decide whether to remove an invasive predatory fish species that has recently established itself in the ecosystem."},"studyGuide":{"rs":"Focus on understanding how biomass calculations account for respiration and time scales. Ensure you can explain why energy efficiency decreases at higher trophic levels due to heat loss. Memorize the definitions of key terms like 'food web' and 'trophic level' in the context of interconnected relationships.","sm":"Review the distinction between energy flow (linear, dissipative) and matter cycling (circular, conserved). food web is simply a longer version of a single linear food chain with more steps.","tr":"Food Web"},{"cx":"Biomass reflects production, consumption, respiration, and time scale, not just organism count.","df":"The total mass of living biological organisms in a given area or ecosystem at a specific time.","eg":"The biomass of phytoplankton in a lake is measured to estimate primary production capacity.","mi":"Biomass is solely determined by the number of individual organisms present in a population.","tr":"Biomass"}],"lessonId":"ald-ecosystem-energy-14","mc":[{"ai":2,"ex":"This directly reflects the principle that matter is conserved and cycled, but energy quality degrades into heat.","fi":[1],"op":["Both energy and matter cycle continuously without loss.","Energy cycles while matter dissipates as heat.","Matter cycles through ecosystems, whereas usable energy dissipates as heat during transfers.","Energy is created by consumers and matter is destroyed by producers."],"q":"Which statement best describes the flow of energy in an ecosystem compared to matter?"},{"ai":1,"ex":"The definition of a food web emphasizes its complexity and interconnectedness over linear simplicity.","fi":[2],"op":["Food webs only include plants and ignore animals.","A food web represents multiple feeding relationships rather than one isolated linear chain.","Food chains are too complex for most ecosystems to support.","Food webs show that energy increases at each trophic level."],"q":"Why is a food web considered a more accurate representation of an ecosystem than a single food chain?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"Producers initiate the energy flow by converting solar radiation into chemical bonds.","fi":[0],"op":["Chemical energy stored in fossil fuels.","Heat generated by geothermal activity.","Sunlight transformed into stored chemical energy by producers.","Kinetic energy from wind and water currents."],"q":"What is the primary source of energy for most ecosystems?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"Ecological networks are interconnected; changes in one node ripple through the system via various pathways.","fi":[3],"op":["No other species will be affected because they do not interact directly.","Only the prey population will increase, with no further effects.","Removing one species can affect several populations through direct and indirect relationships.","Energy will begin to cycle instead of dissipating as heat."],"q":"If a top predator is removed from an ecosystem, what is a likely consequence based on ecological principles?"}],"scenario":{"ma":"Observation 1: The invasive fish preys heavily on native amphibian larvae. Observation 2: Native amphibians are key prey for several bird species. Constraint: Removal methods may also harm non-target bottom-dwelling invertebrates.","su":"A wetland management team must decide whether to remove an invasive predatory fish species that has recently established itself in the ecosystem."},"studyGuide":{"rs":"Focus on understanding how biomass calculations account for respiration and time scales. Ensure you can explain why energy efficiency decreases at higher trophic levels due to heat loss. Memorize the definitions of key terms like 'food web' and 'trophic level' in the context of interconnected relationships.","sm":"Review the distinction between energy flow (linear, dissipative) and matter cycling (circular, conserved). Practice drawing food webs that include decomposers and multiple consumer levels. Analyze case studies where species removal led to trophic cascades."}}]}

{"lessons":[{"assignmentCore":{"pa":["Identify relevant facts","State connecting principles","Test with counterexamples","Assess stakeholder impact"],"td":"Write a 500-word analysis applying two competing principles to the scenario and identify a counterexample for each."},"discussionPrompt":{"po":["Prioritize Patient A based on the principle of maximizing overall health outcomes.","Prioritize Patient B based on the principle of rewarding social contribution.","Use a random selection method to avoid bias, acknowledging uncertainty in moral weighting."],"pr":"How should the committee resolve the conflict between maximizing survival rates and recognizing social value?","tn":"Principle Conflict Analysis"},"facts":["An ethical conclusion depends on both relevant facts and a principle connecting facts to judgment.","Competing principles can support different actions even when people agree on case facts.","A counterexample tests whether a proposed rule gives acceptable judgments in another case.","Stakeholder impact identifies who bears benefits, burdens, risks, and decision authority.","Stating uncertainty prevents a recommendation from appearing more certain than its evidence."],"keyTerms":[{"cx":"It requires both relevant facts and a connecting principle.","df":"A judgment derived from the synthesis of empirical data and normative standards.","eg":"Deciding that lying is wrong because it violates trust.","mi":"It is purely an emotional reaction to a situation.","tr":"Ethical Conclusion"},{"cx":"It tests whether a proposed rule gives acceptable judgments in another case.","df":"A specific case used to test the validity or limits of a general rule.","eg":"Using a life-saving lie to test the rule 'never lie'.","mi":"It proves a rule is universally true without exception.","tr":"Counterexample"},{"cx":"It identifies who bears benefits, burdens, risks, and decision authority.","df":"The analysis of how decisions affect various parties involved.","eg":"Assessing how a new policy affects employees and customers.","mi":"It only considers the financial profit for the company.","tr":"Stakeholder conclusion?"},{"ai":1,"ex":"Competing principles can support different actions even when people agree on case facts.","fi":[1],"op":["They have different factual interpretations","Competing principles support different actions","One person is more emotional","The facts are incomplete"],"q":"Why might people disagree on an action even if they agree on the facts?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"A counterexample tests whether a proposed rule gives acceptable judgments in another case.","fi":[2],"op":["To prove a rule is always correct","To ignore difficult cases","To test if a rule gives acceptable judgments in another case","To simplify the decision process"],"q":"What is the primary function of a counterexample in ethical reasoning?"},{"ai":1,"ex":"Stakeholder impact identifies who bears benefits, burdens, risks, and decision authority.","fi":[3],"op":["Only the financial costs","Who bears benefits, burdens, risks, and decision authority","The legal liability of the decision maker","The {"lessons":[{"assignmentCore":{"pa":["Identify two stocks","Identify two flows","Mark one reinforcing loop","Mark one balancing loop"],"td":"Construct a causal-loop diagram illustrating the relationship between predator and prey populations in a closed ecosystem, identifying at least one balancing loop."},"discussionPrompt":{"po":["Highway expansion is ineffective because positive feedback loops (induced demand) will eventually restore congestion levels.","Highway expansion is necessary because it provides immediate relief and accommodates inevitable population growth.","The focus should be on managing demand through pricing or alternatives, as supply-side solutions ignore the reinforcing nature of driver behavior."],"pr":"Does increasing road capacity effectively solve long-term traffic congestion?","tn":"Induced Demand and System Stability"},"facts":["Negative feedback counteracts a deviation and can stabilize a system near a target.","Positive feedback amplifies an initial change rather than judging the change as beneficial.","A delayed response can cause correction to overshoot the target repeatedly.","A stock accumulates the difference between incoming and outgoing flows over time.","A causal-loop diagram marks reinforcing and balancing relationships without proving magnitude."],"keyTerms":[{"cx":"Negative feedback allows change but acts to correct deviations from a specific target, not to freeze the system in place.","df":"A regulatory mechanism that detects a deviation from a target state and initiates actions to reduce that deviation, thereby stabilizing the system.","eg":"A home thermostat turning off the heating system once the room temperature reaches the desired set point.","mi":"Negative feedback always prevents any change from occurring in the system.","tr":"Negative Feedback"},{"cx":"Positive feedback describes the direction of change (amplification), not its value; it can lead to beneficial growth or harmful runaway effects.","df":"A process where the output of a system reinforces the input, leading to exponential growth or collapse by amplifying initial changes.","eg":"Audio screeching when a microphone picks up sound from its own speaker, causing the volume to increase rapidly.","mi":"Positive feedback implies that the resulting change is morally good or beneficial for the system.","tr":"Positive Feedback"},{"cx":"A stock is dynamic and changes continuously based on the current rates of incoming and outgoing flows.","df":"An accumulation within a system that represents the total quantity built up over time by the net difference between inflows and outflows.","eg":"The water level in a bathtub, which rises if the faucet flow exceeds the drain flow and falls otherwise.","mi":"A stock is a fixed quantity that does not change once the system is established.","tr":"Stock"}],"lessonId":"ald-feedback-loops-16","mc":[{"ai":1,"ex":"Negative feedback counteracts a deviation and can stabilize a system near a target.","fi":[0],"op":["Positive feedback","Negative feedback","Reinforcing loop","Exponential growth"],"q":"Which type of feedback loop helps stabilize a system near a target value?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"Positive feedback amplifies an initial change rather than judging the change as beneficial.","fi":[1],"op":["It stabilizes the system","It reduces deviations","It amplifies an initial change","It proves magnitude"],"q":"What is the primary characteristic of positive feedback?"},{"ai":2,"ex":"A delayed response can cause correction to overshoot the target repeatedly.","fi":[2],"op":["Due to immediate response","Due to lack of feedback","Due to a delayed response","Due to constant inflow"],"q":"Why might a corrective action overshoot the target repeatedly?"},{"ai":1,"ex":"A stock accumulates the difference between incoming and outgoing flows over time.","fi":[3],"op":["It remains constant","It accumulates the difference between incoming and outgoing flows","It only increases with inflow","It only decreases with outflow"],"q":"How does a stock behave over time?"}],"scenario":{"ma":"Decision: Choose between highway expansion and public transit investment. Observation 1: Adding lanes initially reduces travel time per vehicle. Observation 2: Within three years, induced demand causes traffic volume to rise, negating the time savings. Constraint: The budget allows for only one major infrastructure project this decade.","su":"A city planner is addressing chronic traffic congestion on a major highway. The proposed solution is to add two new lanes to increase capacity. However, historical data shows that after similar expansions, traffic volume eventually increased to fill the new space, returning congestion to previous levels. The planner must decide whether to proceed with the expansion or invest in public transit alternatives."},"studyGuide":{"rs":"Focus on understanding that causal-loop diagrams show relationships but do not prove magnitude. Ensure you can identify reinforcing vs. balancing loops in real-world scenarios.","sm":"Review the definitions of negative and positive feedback. Practice distinguishing between stocks (accumulations) and flows (rates). Analyze how delays impact system stability."}}]}

{"lessons":[{"assignmentCore":{"pa":["Identify source type (primary/secondary)","Check population alignment","Assess independence of evidence","Evaluate methodological soundness"],"td":"Analyze two provided sources to determine if they corroborate each other and identify any methodological weaknesses."},"discussionPrompt":{"po":["Researchers should state uncertainty explicitly when evidence cannot distinguish competing explanations.","Researchers should choose the most plausible explanation to avoid confusing the audience.","Researchers should withhold publication until more definitive evidence is available."],"pr":"How should researchers handle situations where available evidence is insufficient to distinguish between competing explanations?","tn":"Transparency in Uncertainty"},"facts":["A claim is stronger when cited evidence directly addresses the same population and outcome.","Publication venue alone cannot establish that a specific claim is methodologically sound.","A primary source records direct evidence, while a secondary source interprets other records.","Corroboration compares independent evidence that bears on the same factual claim.","Uncertainty should be stated when available evidence cannot distinguish competing explanations."],"keyTerms":[{"cx":"A journal article may be a secondary source if it reviews or interprets other studies rather than presenting new data.","df":"A document or record that provides direct, first-hand evidence of an event or phenomenon without interpretation.","eg":"A raw dataset from a clinical trial or a diary entry from a historical figure.","mi":"Any published article in a peer-reviewed journal is automatically a primary source.","tr":"Primary Source"},{"cx":"Corroboration requires independent evidence; relying on the same source repeated elsewhere is not corroboration.","df":"The process of comparing independent pieces of evidence to see if they support the same factual claim.","eg":"Checking if satellite imagery matches eyewitness accounts of a natural disaster.","mi":"Citing multiple sources from the same original study constitutes corroboration.","tr":"Corroboration"},{"cx":"Publication venue alone cannot establish that a specific claim is methodologically sound; the methods must be evaluated directly.","df":"The quality of the research design and execution, which determines the validity of the conclusions drawn.","eg":"Using a randomized control group to isolate the effect of a variable.","mi":"If a study is published in a prestigious venue, its methodology is guaranteed to be sound.","tr":"Methodological Soundness"}],"lessonId":"ald-source-evaluation-17","mc":[{"ai":0,"ex":"Relevance to the specific population and outcome strengthens the direct applicability of the evidence.","fi":[0],"op":["It ensures the evidence is relevant and applicable to the specific context of the claim.","It guarantees that the publication venue is prestigious.","It allows the researcher to ignore secondary sources entirely.","It eliminates the need for corroboration from independent sources."],"q":"Which statement best describes why a claim is stronger when cited evidence directly addresses the same population and outcome?"},{"ai":0,"ex":"The prestige of the venue does not validate the specific methods used in a study.","fi":[1],"op":["Because publication venue alone cannot establish that a specific claim is methodologically sound.","Because primary sources are never published in high-impact journals.","Because all journals have the same editorial standards.","Because secondary sources are always more reliable than primary ones."],"q":"Why is relying solely on the publication venue insufficient for evaluating a claim?"},{"ai":0,"ex":"Primary sources contain original data or direct accounts, whereas secondary sources analyze or interpret those records.","fi":[2],"op":["A primary source records direct evidence, while a secondary source interprets other records.","A primary source is always older than a secondary source.","A secondary source provides raw data, while a primary source provides analysis.","There is no difference; both terms refer to the same type of document."],"q":"What is the key difference between a primary source and a secondary source?"},{"ai":0,"ex":"Corroboration requires independent lines of evidence to support the same conclusion, increasing confidence.","fi":[3],"op":["It compares independent evidence that bears on the same factual claim.","It relies on multiple citations from the same single study.","It ensures that the publication venue is highly ranked.","It removes the need to state uncertainty in the findings."],"q":"How does corroboration strengthen a factual claim?"}],"scenario":{"ma":"Observation 1: A high-impact journal published a study supporting the intervention. Observation 2: A primary data audit reveals the study's sample population differs significantly from the target demographic. Constraint: The team must justify their decision using evidence evaluation principles rather than prestige.","su":"A policy team must decide whether to fund a new public health intervention based on conflicting reports."},"studyGuide":{"rs":"Focus on how corroboration requires independence and why uncertainty must be explicitly stated when evidence is inconclusive.","sm":"Review the definitions of primary vs. secondary sources and practice identifying methodological flaws in sample populations."}}]}

{"lessons":[{"assignmentCore":{"pa":["input_sizes","repetitions_per_size","warmup_iterations","output_format"],"td":"Implement both linear and binary search functions, then write a script to benchmark their execution times across input sizes from 1 to 1000."},"discussionPrompt":{"po":["Asymptotic analysis should always dictate choice because it guarantees scalability if the data grows unexpectedly.","Empirical benchmarks are superior because constant factors and hardware specifics dominate performance on small inputs.","A hybrid approach is best: use simple algorithms for small N and switch to asymptotically efficient ones only when N exceeds a threshold."],"pr":"Should engineering teams prioritize asymptotic efficiency or empirical benchmark performance when selecting algorithms for small-scale data processing?","tn":"The tension between theoretical optimality and practical runtime efficiency in constrained environments."},"facts":["Asymptotic analysis compares growth rates while ignoring constant factors at sufficiently large input sizes.","Binary search requires an ordered search space and halves the remaining interval each step.","A linear scan may outperform binary search on tiny inputs despite its less favorable growth rate.","Worst-case complexity bounds the maximum work over inputs of a given size.","Benchmark results depend on implementation, hardware, input distribution, and measurement procedure."],"keyTerms":[{"cx":"Asymptotic analysis ignores constant factors and hardware specifics, focusing only on growth rates for large inputs.","df":"A method for comparing algorithm efficiency by examining how resource usage scales with input size as it approaches infinity, disregarding fixed overheads.","eg":"Comparing O(n) vs O(log n) to determine which algorithm handles larger datasets more efficiently in the long run.","mi":"Asymptotic analysis provides the exact runtime in milliseconds for any specific input size on a given machine.","tr":"Asymptotic Analysis"},{"cx":"Linear scan may outperform binary search on tiny inputs due to lower constant factors and overhead despite worse asymptotic growth.","df":"An efficient search algorithm that operates on sorted arrays by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half to locate a target value.","eg":"Finding a name in a sorted phone book by opening to the middle, checking the name, and discarding the irrelevant half.","mi":"Binary search is always faster than linear search because it has a better worst-case time complexity class.","tr":"Binary Search"},{"cx":"Worst-case complexity bounds the maximum work over inputs of a given size, not the average or best-case scenarios.","df":"The upper bound on the amount of work or time an algorithm requires for any possible input of a specified size.","eg":"Stating that a sorting algorithm takes at most O(n^2) steps ensures it will never exceed this limit regardless of input order.","mi":"Worst-case complexity represents the average performance of an algorithm across all typical real-world inputs.","tr":"Worst-Case
